subject: ap-chemistry
title: Manganese Chemistry: Electron Configuration, Empirical Formula & Electrochemistry
stimulus: 1. Answer the following questions related to manganese compounds.

parts.prompt:
Manganese has several common oxidation states.
(i)Write the complete electron configuration for an Mn atom in the ground state.
(ii)When manganese forms cations, electrons are lost from which subshell first? Identify both the number
and letter associated with the subshell.
A student performs an experiment to produce a manganese salt of unknown composition, Mn Cl (aq), and
x y
determine its empirical formula. The student places a sample of Mn(s) in a beaker containing excess HCl(aq), as
represented by the following equation.
y
x Mn(s) + y HCl(aq) → Mn Cl (aq) + H (g)
x y 2

The student heats the resulting mixture until only Mn Cl (s) remains in the beaker. The data are given in the
x y
following table.
Mass of empty beaker 60.169 g
Mass of beaker and Mn(s) 61.262 g
Mass of beaker and Mn Cl after heating to constant mass 62.673 g
x y
parts.scoring_notes: Part (a)(i): accept noble gas notation [Ar]4s²3d⁵ or full configuration. Part (a)(ii): 4s — transition metals lose 4s electrons before 3d.

scoring_points.description: For the correct ground-state electron configuration of Mn: 1 point
wrong_examples: $[\text{Ar}]4s^2 3d^5$ — this is equivalent and acceptable; order of subshells in notation is flexible.
$[\text{Ar}]3d^6 4s^1$ — Mn does not have a half-filled 4s exception; the 3d⁵ 4s² configuration is correct.
common_traps: Mn (Z=25) is in period 4, group 7; [Ar] core (18e⁻) + 3d⁵ + 4s² = 25 electrons total.

scoring_points.description: For identifying the 4s subshell as the first to lose electrons: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: When transition metals form cations, electrons are removed from the highest principal quantum number (n) first. For Mn, the 4s subshell (n=4) electrons are lost before the 3d subshell (n=3) electrons.

wrong_examples: 3d — 4s electrons are lost first because they are in a higher energy shell (higher n) in the ion context.
common_traps: Although 3d fills after 4s, 4s electrons are removed first when forming cations — standard exam rule.

parts.prompt:
Calculate the mass of Cl in the sample of Mn Cl (s) remaining in the beaker.
x y

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated mass of Cl: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using 62.673 − 61.262 = 1.411 g — this is a shortcut that happens to be correct in this specific case; accept it.
Forgetting to subtract the empty beaker mass.
common_traps: Mass of Cl = mass of MnCl salt − mass of Mn = (beaker+salt) − (beaker+Mn) = 62.673 − 61.262.

parts.prompt:
Calculate the number of moles of Cl in the sample of Mn Cl (s) remaining in the beaker.
x y

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated moles of Cl, consistent with part (b): 1 point
wrong_examples: Dividing by 70.90 g/mol (Cl₂ molar mass) instead of 35.45 (Cl atomic mass) — Cl is an atom here, not a diatomic molecule.
common_traps: The salt contains Cl atoms (chloride ions), not Cl₂ molecules; use atomic mass 35.45 g/mol.

parts.prompt:
The student determines that 0.0199 mol of Mn was used in the experiment. Use the data to determine the
empirical formula of the Mn Cl (s).
x y

scoring_points.description: For the correct empirical formula MnCl₂: 1 point
wrong_examples: MnCl₃ — the ratio comes out to 2.0, not 3.0.
common_traps: Round the mole ratio to the nearest whole number to determine the empirical formula.

parts.prompt:
The student repeats the experiment using the same amounts of Mn and HCl and notices that some of the
Mn Cl splatters out of the beaker as it is heated to dryness. Will the number of moles of Cl calculated for
x y
this trial be greater than, less than, or equal to the number calculated in part (c) ? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Less than — splattering removes some MnCl₂, reducing the final mass, which decreases the apparent Cl mass, which decreases calculated moles of Cl.

scoring_points.description: For 'less than' with valid justification: 1 point

required_elements: Some MnCl₂ is lost due to splattering, so the final mass of the beaker + salt is lower than expected.
The calculated mass and moles of Cl will be less than in part (c).
alternatives.correct_example: Less than. Some MnClₓ splatters out, so the final recorded mass of the beaker + salt is lower than it should be. The calculated mass of Cl = (beaker + salt) − (beaker + Mn) is reduced, and therefore the calculated moles of Cl is also reduced.

wrong_examples: Greater than — losing salt decreases the measured mass, not increases it.
common_traps: Mass of Cl is calculated from the difference of two masses; if the final mass is artificially low, Cl mass is underestimated.

parts.prompt:
Another compound of manganese, MnO , is used in alkaline batteries, represented by the following diagram.
Some half-reactions are given in the table.
(i)Based on the half-reactions given in the table, write the balanced net ionic equation for the reaction that
has the greatest thermodynamic favorability.
(ii)Calculate the value of E c ell for the overall reaction.
(iii)Calculate the value of ΔG° in kJ/mol .
rxn
(iv)A student claims that the total mass of an alkaline battery decreases as the battery operates because the
anode loses mass. Do you agree with the student's claim? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Half-reactions (from alkaline battery): MnO₂(s) + H₂O(l) + e⁻ → MnO(OH)(s) + OH⁻(aq) E° = +0.15 V (cathode); Zn(s) + 2OH⁻(aq) → ZnO(s) + H₂O(l) + 2e⁻ E° = +1.28 V (anode, reversed). E°_cell = 0.15 + 1.28 = 1.43 V. ΔG° = −nFE° = −(2)(96485)(1.43) = −276 kJ/mol. Part (iv): disagree — mass lost at anode (Zn) is balanced by mass gained at cathode (MnO₂ → MnOOH) plus OH⁻ consumed; total battery mass is conserved in a closed system.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net ionic equation: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using non-alkaline (acidic) half-reactions — alkaline batteries operate in basic (OH⁻) medium.
common_traps: Reference the half-reactions provided in the diagram on the exam; write only the equation with the greatest E°_cell.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated E°_cell consistent with part (f)(i): 1 point
wrong_examples: Subtracting both as reductions without reversing the sign of the anode.
common_traps: $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{red,cathode}} - E°_{\text{red,anode}}$; Zn anode E°_red = −1.28 V.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated ΔG° in kJ/mol, consistent with E°_cell: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using n = 1 — the balanced equation transfers 2 electrons.
Forgetting to convert J to kJ.
common_traps: $\Delta G° = -nFE°$; n = number of moles of electrons in the balanced equation.

scoring_points.description: For a correct answer (disagree) with valid conservation of mass justification: 1 point

required_elements: The anode does lose mass, but the cathode gains mass. In a sealed battery, mass is conserved — the total mass does not decrease.
alternatives.correct_example: Disagree. While the Zn anode does lose mass as Zn is oxidized to ZnO, the MnO₂ cathode gains mass as it is reduced to MnO(OH). Since the battery is a closed system, mass is conserved — the total mass of the battery does not change. The student's claim is only partially correct.

wrong_examples: Agree without qualification — the anode loses mass, but total battery mass is conserved.
common_traps: In electrochemistry, mass lost at one electrode is compensated by mass gained (or species consumed) at the other electrode in a closed cell.
